# Axona Chat — Implementation Addendum

**Companion to:** `Axona_Chat_Design_v0_3.md`
**Addendum version:** 0.1
**Status:** Change requests against the first working build (generated by Google Antigravity/Gemini from the v0.3 design).

---

## How to read this document

The v0.3 design document describes the **intended** application. A working version now exists, and for the most part it tracks the design. This addendum is **not** a re-description of the design and **not** a diff you need to compute. It is a direct, itemized list of **changes to make to the existing application** — the places where the built version is incomplete, unclear as-built, buggy, or where using the live app revealed a better approach than the design specified.

Each item is written as a self-contained change request. Where an item relates to a section of the design doc, the section is cited as `[v0.3 §N]` so it can be located, but you do **not** need to re-read the whole design to act on any item here. Act on the items directly.

This is the first pass. The process is iterative; expect follow-up addenda after each round of changes.

**Priority key:**
`[P1]` — core interaction or visible bug, do first.
`[P2]` — significant improvement, do this pass.
`[P3]` — new capability or forward-looking; design/implement this pass if straightforward, otherwise note and discuss.

---

## A. Composer / message entry

### A1. `[P1]` Expand the composer into an overlay while editing — `[v0.3 §7.2]`

**Current:** The markdown entry field at the bottom of the topic view is a small, fixed, single-line-ish text area. It enables markdown, but the editing area is too small to see the formatting or flow of anything longer than a sentence.

**Change:** Clicking into the bottom entry field should grow it into a large pop-up / overlay editing surface that covers most of the app, giving a full text-area with room to see the rendered formatting and flow while composing. Clicking anywhere outside the overlay collapses it back down to the small bottom entry field, which still shows the text currently being composed (draft is preserved, not discarded). The user can freely toggle between the two: click into the field → it zooms up into the overlay; click away → it drops back to the compact bar so the message list is unobstructed.

**Intent:** Make it fast to switch between reading the conversation and composing a longer, well-formatted message, without the editor ever being in the way of the messages.

**Keep:** The existing WYSIWYG-markdown behavior, the raw-toggle, and the 15 KB cap from the design all still apply inside the expanded overlay.

---

## B. Voice input and output

### B1. `[P3]` Add voice input (speech-to-text) and voice output (text-to-speech) — *new, not in v0.3*

**Change:** Add two voice capabilities:

- **Voice input:** dictate a message by voice; the app transcribes speech to text into the composer.
- **Voice output:** the app can read the messages in a topic aloud (text-to-speech), so a user can listen to the conversation rather than read it.

**Status / approach:** This is a **day-one nice-to-have** — highly desirable, but the implementation approach is **not yet decided** and is explicitly open for discussion. Do not lock an approach without raising options first. The likely first cut is the browser-native Web Speech API (`SpeechRecognition` for input, `SpeechSynthesis` for output) since it requires no backend and fits the serverless model, but this needs to be weighed (browser support, quality, offline behavior) before committing. Treat this item as "reserve the UI space and flag the design decision," not "ship a specific implementation blindly."

---

## C. Topic metadata display

### C1. `[P2]` Surface topic metrics in the topic view — `[v0.3 §12]`

**Current:** When a topic is displayed, the UI shows whether it is open, but not its activity. The metrics capability exists in the model but is not surfaced.

**Change:** When viewing a topic, display its metrics/metadata near the topic name — at minimum the **message count** available in the topic, drawn from the kernel's `metricTopic(T)` data. Include other useful topic metadata alongside it where available (e.g. mode/open state, activity/presence indicators).

**Honor the design's metrics semantics `[v0.3 §12]`:** silence from the metric channel means **UNKNOWN**, not zero — do not render an absent metric as "0 messages." Only an explicit `current_count: 0` means genuinely quiet/empty. Render the UNKNOWN state distinctly (e.g. "—" or "…"), never as a hard zero.

---

## D. Discover ticker (advertised topics)

### D1. `[P1]` Pause on hover and make the whole ad clickable — `[v0.3 §5]`

**Current:** The "Discover" ticker at the top scrolls a list of advertised topics. Advertising works (clicking **Advertise** posts an ad for the current topic; the ad — e.g. "This is my advertisement for lobby" — then appears in the ticker). But the ticker keeps scrolling under the cursor, and only the trailing **Open** button is clickable.

**Change:**
1. **Pause on hover:** when the cursor is over the ticker (or over an individual ad), the scrolling pauses, so the ad can be read and targeted.
2. **Whole-ad click target:** the entire advertised-topic string — the topic name, its description text, and the trailing button region — becomes a single clickable target that joins/opens that topic. Clicking anywhere on the ad joins it, not just the **Open** button.

**Keep:** "Discover" is a good name for this surface — retain it. The no-self-advertising rule and hide-to-unsubscribe behavior from the design still stand.

---

## E. Regions — open architectural question

### E1. `[P3 / DISCUSS]` Decide the region strategy; current single-region default is acceptable for MLP — `[v0.3 §4, §13]`

**Current (as built):** Topics do not display a region, and everything appears to be placed in a **single region** (US East). For North America at MLP scale this is very likely fine — East Coast node density should be sufficient to carry the app — and it has a real UX benefit: **the user never has to think about regions.**

**The tradeoff to resolve (not a bug — a design decision):** Hiding regions and defaulting everyone to one region maximizes simplicity but concentrates hosting/replication load. If the app becomes very successful, the participants in the single default region (East) bear a disproportionate share of the work of hosting everyone's topics, while node-sparse areas (much of the middle of the country) contribute little.

**Options to weigh (for discussion, no decision baked in yet):**
1. **Single default region (current).** Simplest; user never sees regions. Accept geographic load concentration as a scaling problem to solve later.
2. **Transparent multi-region.** Distribute topics across regions automatically (by heuristic/proximity) with **regions still hidden from the user**. Spreads load; adds routing complexity and potential latency variability.
3. **Replicate-to-multiple, default-to-nearest.** Topics live in more than one region; the user is silently attached to the nearest, preserving the "no region UI" experience while improving fault tolerance.

**For this pass:** keep the single-region default and keep regions out of the UI. Flag this as an explicit open decision to revisit before any scale push. Do **not** add a user-facing region picker.

---

## F. Personas and the human/agent declaration

### F1. `[P1]` Fix human/agent toggle layout overflow — `[v0.3 §6]`

**Current:** The declaration control (the "Human operator" ⇄ "AI agent" toggle) is visually broken: the toggle element **overflows to the right, off the edge of its tile.** The toggle sits outside the container that labels it.

**Change:** Fix the layout so the toggle is fully contained within its "Human operator / AI agent" tile, properly aligned, with no overflow. This is purely a visual/layout fix; the toggle's behavior (switching the operator declaration globally, per §6) is correct and unchanged.

### F2. `[P1]` Fix persona-creation dialog z-order — `[v0.3 §6]`

**Current:** First-launch persona generation works correctly (the app requires at least one persona and offers a default name, e.g. "Alice"). But creating an **additional** persona is difficult because the persona-creation pop-up renders **underneath the human-operator toggle** — the toggle sits on top of the dialog, obscuring it.

**Change:** Fix stacking order so the persona-creation dialog renders **above** all header/toggle chrome (correct z-index / modal layering). The dialog must be fully visible and interactive when open. Confirm the create-additional-persona flow is reachable and usable once the layering is fixed.

---

## G. App/protocol identity and sharing

### G1. `[P2]` Make the "Axona Chat vX.Y" header clickable → about/protocol modal — *new* — `[v0.3 §14]`

**Current:** The top-left shows "Axona Chat — peer-to-peer v0.3" (mirroring the design doc) but it is not interactive.

**Change:** Make that title clickable. Clicking it opens a modal that:
- describes the **Axona Chat** application, and
- advertises the **Axona protocol** underneath it, with a link to **axona.net** where an interested user can explore the protocol.

**Intent:** Every running instance quietly evangelizes the protocol. Keep the copy in the conservative collective register used elsewhere.

### G2. `[P3]` QR code → app share + private encrypted channel — *new capability* — `[builds on v0.3 §9]`

This is the most substantial new item. It turns the design's private-encrypted-reply mechanism (§9) into a **shareable relationship primitive** via a QR code.

**Placement:** A small QR code somewhere in the chrome (top-left, top-right, or bottom corner). Clicking it enlarges it for scanning/sharing; it can be dismissed instantly.

**What the QR code encodes:**
1. A link that **gets the other person the app** (takes a new user to the application; an existing user is simply brought into the app).
2. A **random private topic ID** (generated per the §9 random-topic mechanism).
3. A **shared key** (shared key material / seed) enabling a secure, encrypted connection in that topic.

**The relationship model this creates:**
- Scanning the QR code drops both people into the **same shared topic** over the ordinary publish/subscribe infrastructure. The topic is an ordinary **open** topic — anyone could see that traffic exists — **but only the two parties hold the keys**, so the messages are **encrypted end-to-end between them** and unreadable to anyone else. This is exactly the §9 model ("publish without subscribe" / encrypt-to-recipient), surfaced as a persistent one-to-one channel rather than a one-off reply.
- **The QR code is always the private channel.** It is not a mode selector; there is one QR behavior and it is "connect privately with me."

**Local naming of the channel (important UX detail):**
- The topic's real ID is random/opaque. Each user needs a human-friendly cue for **who they are talking to.**
- On connecting, **each party gets a pop-up to name the connection locally.** This label is **local-only** — it is a personal reference held only by that user, **not** the topic ID and not shared over the network. The two users' labels are independent (David labels it "Alice"; Alice labels it "David"); that is expected and fine.
- The label is **editable later** — it is just a personal mnemonic, so renaming has no network effect.
- In the topic list, each user sees their own label for the channel so they can tell these private channels apart.

**Relationship management — note the gap:** The app currently has **no model for managing relationships to other people** (no contacts/follow concept). The QR-code-plus-private-channel pattern is effectively our first relationship primitive: the relationship *is* the shared encrypted topic. Note this as a deliberate direction and a place to expand later (e.g. a list of these private channels as a lightweight "contacts" surface), but for this pass, the single private channel per QR exchange is the model.

**Forward idea (not required this pass):** a QR code could additionally carry the *currently viewed topic* so scanning also offers to join, say, "Lobby" instantly. For v0.1 of this feature, keep the QR code scoped to **app + private channel**; the topic-join variant can come later. (Removing any joined topic is already frictionless — every topic has an ✕ — so there's low risk in topic-carrying QR later.)

---

## H. Theming

### H1. `[P2]` Add a light/dark theme toggle — *new* — `[v0.3 §7.4 styling]`

**Current:** The build is dark mode only (light-on-black).

**Change:** Let the user switch between dark and light themes. The current dark look is good and can remain the default, but many users read black-on-white far more comfortably, so a toggle is needed. Follow the standard pattern: theme is applied from stored preference on load (no flash of wrong theme), and swapping themes swaps design-token variable values rather than re-rendering (consistent with the token-based styling approach). Persist the choice locally.

---

## I. Layout reorganization (left-to-right simplification)

### I1. `[P1]` Push metadata/status chrome to the bottom edges; give topics and message text the full width — *new* — `[v0.3 §15]`

**Current layout (four columns, left→right):**
1. Far left: "Axona Chat" + active persona, and beneath it the human-operator declaration; bottom-left has connection status (online, which bridge, DHT node ID).
2. Next: **Topics** list.
3. Center: selected topic + message text.
4. Right: participants (humans/agents).

**Problem:** Too much metadata occupies prime horizontal space, squeezing the two things that actually matter — the **topics list** and the **topic message text**.

**Target layout:**
- **Move all movable metadata/chrome to the bottom.** The persona/declaration/connection-status cluster moves to the **bottom-left** — effectively next to the compact text-entry field. Think of this metadata as living along the bottom edge rather than down the left side.
- **Topics list moves to the far-left edge** and takes the leftmost column.
- **Participants list (humans/agents) moves down to the bottom-right.**
- **Result:** the main area is dominated by just two things — the **topics list (far left)** and the **selected topic's message text** — spanning from far left to far right, with metadata and status tucked along the bottom edges. Simplify the layout to this shape.

### I2. `[P2]` Simplify the connection-status display — `[v0.3 §4]`

Within the relocated status area:

- **Drop the DHT node ID from the UI.** It changes on essentially every load (transport/node identity is per-session per §4) and is not meaningful to a user. Remove it. *(If any node/DHT identifier is ever shown anywhere in the app, it must be rendered in **hex**, never decimal — DHT values shown in decimal are meaningless. But the preference here is simply not to show it.)*
- **Bridge display is low value:** the connected bridge can change on every load and isn't important to the user; keep the status focused on the actual **connection state** (online/offline) rather than emphasizing which bridge or which node ID.

---

## J. Consolidated change checklist (for quick reference)

| # | Area | Change | Priority |
|---|------|--------|----------|
| A1 | Composer | Click-to-expand overlay editor; collapse on click-away; preserve draft | P1 |
| B1 | Voice | Add STT input + TTS output; approach TBD (likely Web Speech API) | P3 |
| C1 | Metrics | Show message count + metadata by topic name; UNKNOWN ≠ 0 | P2 |
| D1 | Discover | Pause on hover; entire ad clickable to join | P1 |
| E1 | Regions | Keep single-region default, no region UI; flag as open decision | P3/discuss |
| F1 | Personas | Fix human/agent toggle overflow off its tile | P1 |
| F2 | Personas | Fix persona-creation dialog z-order (renders under toggle) | P1 |
| G1 | Identity | Clickable app-title → about + Axona protocol modal → axona.net | P2 |
| G2 | Sharing | QR code = app link + private encrypted channel; local editable labels | P3 |
| H1 | Theming | Light/dark toggle; dark stays default; persist choice | P2 |
| I1 | Layout | Metadata/participants to bottom edges; topics far-left; text full-width | P1 |
| I2 | Status | Drop DHT node ID (hex-only if ever shown); de-emphasize bridge | P2 |

---

## K. Notes on process

- These are **changes to the existing application**, not a fresh build. Apply them against the current version; do not regenerate from scratch and do not re-derive intent from the full design doc.
- Where an item cites `[v0.3 §N]`, that is a pointer for context only. The change is fully specified here.
- This is an **iterative** addendum. After this pass lands, the next round will build on the result. Expect `Implementation_Addendum_v0_2`, etc.
- Two items are explicitly **open for discussion before locking** — voice approach (B1) and region strategy (E1). Raise options rather than committing silently.
